<feed xmlns:opensearch="http://a9.com/-/spec/opensearch/1.1/" xmlns:arxiv="http://arxiv.org/schemas/atom" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2005/Atom">
  <id>https://arxiv.org/api/TzrbIrrdnAItVt7yfpMteSy1R9w</id>
  <title>arXiv Query: search_query=ti:"BoNBoN Alignment for Large Language Models"&id_list=&start=0&max_results=3</title>
  <updated>2026-03-23T18:17:46Z</updated>
  <link href="https://arxiv.org/api/query?search_query=ti:%22BoNBoN+Alignment+for+Large+Language+Models%22&start=0&max_results=3&id_list=" type="application/atom+xml"/>
  <opensearch:itemsPerPage>3</opensearch:itemsPerPage>
  <opensearch:totalResults>1</opensearch:totalResults>
  <opensearch:startIndex>0</opensearch:startIndex>
  <entry>
    <id>http://arxiv.org/abs/2406.00832v3</id>
    <title>BoNBoN Alignment for Large Language Models and the Sweetness of Best-of-n Sampling</title>
    <updated>2024-11-01T20:02:32Z</updated>
    <link href="https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.00832v3" rel="alternate" type="text/html"/>
    <link href="https://arxiv.org/pdf/2406.00832v3" rel="related" type="application/pdf" title="pdf"/>
    <summary>This paper concerns the problem of aligning samples from large language models to human preferences using best-of-$n$ sampling, where we draw $n$ samples, rank them, and return the best one. We consider two fundamental problems. First: what is the relationship between best-of-$n$ and approaches to alignment that train LLMs to output samples with a high expected reward (e.g., RLHF or DPO)? To answer this, we embed both the best-of-$n$ distribution and the sampling distributions learned by alignment procedures in a common class of tiltings of the base LLM distribution. We then show that, within this class, best-of-$n$ is essentially optimal in terms of the trade-off between win-rate against the base model vs KL distance from the base model. That is, best-of-$n$ is the best choice of alignment distribution if the goal is to maximize win rate. However, best-of-$n$ requires drawing $n$ samples for each inference, a substantial cost. To avoid this, the second problem we consider is how to fine-tune a LLM to mimic the best-of-$n$ sampling distribution. We derive BoNBoN Alignment to achieve this by exploiting the special structure of the best-of-$n$ distribution. Experiments show that BoNBoN alignment yields substantial improvements in producing a model that is preferred to the base policy while minimally affecting off-target aspects.</summary>
    <category term="cs.CL" scheme="http://arxiv.org/schemas/atom"/>
    <category term="cs.LG" scheme="http://arxiv.org/schemas/atom"/>
    <published>2024-06-02T18:42:57Z</published>
    <arxiv:primary_category term="cs.CL"/>

    </author>
    <author>
      <name>Cristina Gârbacea</name>
    </author>

    </author>

<?xml version='1.0' encoding='UTF-8'?>
<feed xmlns:opensearch="http://a9.com/-/spec/opensearch/1.1/" xmlns:arxiv="http://arxiv.org/schemas/atom" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2005/Atom">
  <id>https://arxiv.org/api/I6W9nNCcjgZJdg1RYNNVzI/uFvA</id>
  <title>arXiv Query: search_query=ti:"Asymptotics of Language Model Alignment"&id_list=&start=0&max_results=3</title>
  <updated>2026-03-23T18:17:47Z</updated>
  <link href="https://arxiv.org/api/query?search_query=ti:%22Asymptotics+of+Language+Model+Alignment%22&start=0&max_results=3&id_list=" type="application/atom+xml"/>
  <opensearch:itemsPerPage>3</opensearch:itemsPerPage>
  <opensearch:totalResults>1</opensearch:totalResults>
  <opensearch:startIndex>0</opensearch:startIndex>
  <entry>
    <id>http://arxiv.org/abs/2404.01730v1</id>
    <title>Asymptotics of Language Model Alignment</title>
    <updated>2024-04-02T08:40:07Z</updated>
    <link href="https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.01730v1" rel="alternate" type="text/html"/>
    <link href="https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.01730v1" rel="related" type="application/pdf" title="pdf"/>
    <summary>Let $p$ denote a generative language model. Let $r$ denote a reward model that returns a scalar that captures the degree at which a draw from $p$ is preferred. The goal of language model alignment is to alter $p$ to a new distribution $φ$ that results in a higher expected reward while keeping $φ$ close to $p.$ A popular alignment method is the KL-constrained reinforcement learning (RL), which chooses a distribution $φ_Δ$ that maximizes $E_{φ_Δ} r(y)$ subject to a relative entropy constraint $KL(φ_Δ|| p) \leq Δ.$ Another simple alignment method is best-of-$N$, where $N$ samples are drawn from $p$ and one with highest reward is selected. In this paper, we offer a closed-form characterization of the optimal KL-constrained RL solution. We demonstrate that any alignment method that achieves a comparable trade-off between KL divergence and reward must approximate the optimal KL-constrained RL solution in terms of relative entropy. To further analyze the properties of alignment methods, we introduce two simplifying assumptions: we let the language model be memoryless, and the reward model be linear. Although these assumptions may not reflect complex real-world scenarios, they enable a precise characterization of the asymptotic behavior of both the best-of-$N$ alignment, and the KL-constrained RL method, in terms of information-theoretic quantities. We prove that the reward of the optimal KL-constrained RL solution satisfies a large deviation principle, and we fully characterize its rate function. We also show that the rate of growth of the scaled cumulants of the reward is characterized by a proper Renyi cross entropy. Finally, we show that best-of-$N$ is asymptotically equivalent to KL-constrained RL solution by proving that their expected rewards are asymptotically equal, and concluding that the two distributions must be close in KL divergence.</summary>
    <category term="cs.LG" scheme="http://arxiv.org/schemas/atom"/>
    <category term="cs.IT" scheme="http://arxiv.org/schemas/atom"/>
    <category term="stat.ML" scheme="http://arxiv.org/schemas/atom"/>
    <published>2024-04-02T08:40:07Z</published>
    <arxiv:primary_category term="cs.LG"/>
    <author>
      <name>Joy Qiping Yang</name>
    </author>
    <author>
      <name>Salman Salamatian</name>
    </author>
    <author>
      <name>Ziteng Sun</name>
    </author>

<feed xmlns:opensearch="http://a9.com/-/spec/opensearch/1.1/" xmlns:arxiv="http://arxiv.org/schemas/atom" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2005/Atom">
  <id>https://arxiv.org/api/ydRshAGHluARsiUdX71/6V6UBpI</id>
  <title>arXiv Query: search_query=ti:"Correcting the Mythos of KL-Regularization"&id_list=&start=0&max_results=3</title>
  <updated>2026-03-23T18:17:51Z</updated>
  <link href="https://arxiv.org/api/query?search_query=ti:%22Correcting+the+Mythos+of+KL-Regularization%22&start=0&max_results=3&id_list=" type="application/atom+xml"/>
  <opensearch:itemsPerPage>3</opensearch:itemsPerPage>
  <opensearch:totalResults>1</opensearch:totalResults>
  <opensearch:startIndex>0</opensearch:startIndex>
  <entry>
    <id>http://arxiv.org/abs/2407.13399v3</id>
    <title>Correcting the Mythos of KL-Regularization: Direct Alignment without Overoptimization via Chi-Squared Preference Optimization</title>
    <updated>2025-02-18T17:16:55Z</updated>
    <link href="https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.13399v3" rel="alternate" type="text/html"/>
    <link href="https://arxiv.org/pdf/2407.13399v3" rel="related" type="application/pdf" title="pdf"/>
    <summary>Language model alignment methods such as reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) have led to impressive advances in language model capabilities, but are limited by a widely observed phenomenon known as overoptimization, where the quality of the language model degrades over the course of the alignment process. As the model optimizes performance with respect to an offline reward model, it overfits to inaccuracies and drifts away from preferred responses covered by the data. To discourage such distribution shift, KL-regularization is widely employed in existing offline alignment methods, but overoptimization continues to harm performance. Lending theoretical insight into the source of these empirical observations, we first show that the KL-regularization is too weak to prevent overfitting, then raise the following question: is it possible to design an efficient algorithm that is provably robust to overoptimization?
  We address this question with a new algorithm for offline alignment, $χ^2$-Preference Optimization ($χ$PO). $χ$PO is a one-line change to Direct Preference Optimization (DPO; Rafailov et al., 2023), which only involves modifying the logarithmic link function in the DPO objective. Despite this minimal change, $χ$PO implicitly implements the principle of pessimism in the face of uncertainty via regularization with the $χ^2$-divergence -- which quantifies uncertainty more effectively than KL-regularization -- and provably alleviates overoptimization, achieving sample-complexity guarantees based on single-policy concentrability -- the gold standard in offline reinforcement learning. $χ$PO's simplicity and strong guarantees make it the first practical and general-purpose offline alignment algorithm that is provably robust to overoptimization.</summary>
    <category term="cs.AI" scheme="http://arxiv.org/schemas/atom"/>
    <category term="cs.CL" scheme="http://arxiv.org/schemas/atom"/>
    <category term="cs.LG" scheme="http://arxiv.org/schemas/atom"/>
    <published>2024-07-18T11:08:40Z</published>
    <arxiv:primary_category term="cs.AI"/>
    <author>
      <name>Audrey Huang</name>
    </author>
    <author>
      <name>Wenhao Zhan</name>
    </author>
    <author>
      <name>Tengyang Xie</name>

<feed xmlns:opensearch="http://a9.com/-/spec/opensearch/1.1/" xmlns:arxiv="http://arxiv.org/schemas/atom" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2005/Atom">
  <id>https://arxiv.org/api/lkYpMDyf38tTEsf4W7JBBEBoGpE</id>
  <title>arXiv Query: search_query=ti:"Creativity Has Left the Chat" AND au:Mohammadi&id_list=&start=0&max_results=3</title>
  <updated>2026-03-23T18:17:52Z</updated>
  <link href="https://arxiv.org/api/query?search_query=ti:%22Creativity+Has+Left+the+Chat%22+AND+au:Mohammadi&start=0&max_results=3&id_list=" type="application/atom+xml"/>

  <opensearch:startIndex>0</opensearch:startIndex>
  <entry>
    <id>http://arxiv.org/abs/2406.05587v1</id>
    <title>Creativity Has Left the Chat: The Price of Debiasing Language Models</title>
    <updated>2024-06-08T22:14:51Z</updated>
    <link href="https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.05587v1" rel="alternate" type="text/html"/>
    <link href="https://arxiv.org/pdf/2406.05587v1" rel="related" type="application/pdf" title="pdf"/>
    <summary>Large Language Models (LLMs) have revolutionized natural language processing but can exhibit biases and may generate toxic content. While alignment techniques like Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) reduce these issues, their impact on creativity, defined as syntactic and semantic diversity, remains unexplored. We investigate the unintended consequences of RLHF on the creativity of LLMs through three experiments focusing on the Llama-2 series. Our findings reveal that aligned models exhibit lower entropy in token predictions, form distinct clusters in the embedding space, and gravitate towards "attractor states", indicating limited output diversity. Our findings have significant implications for marketers who rely on LLMs for creative tasks such as copywriting, ad creation, and customer persona generation. The trade-off between consistency and creativity in aligned models should be carefully considered when selecting the appropriate model for a given application. We also discuss the importance of prompt engineering in harnessing the creative potential of base models.</summary>
    <category term="cs.CL" scheme="http://arxiv.org/schemas/atom"/>
    <category term="cs.AI" scheme="http://arxiv.org/schemas/atom"/>
    <published>2024-06-08T22:14:51Z</published>
    <arxiv:primary_category term="cs.CL"/>
    <author>
      <name>Behnam Mohammadi</name>
    </author>
  </entry>
</feed>

<feed xmlns:opensearch="http://a9.com/-/spec/opensearch/1.1/" xmlns:arxiv="http://arxiv.org/schemas/atom" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2005/Atom">

  <updated>2026-03-23T18:17:53Z</updated>
  <link href="https://arxiv.org/api/query?search_query=ti:%22One+fish+two+fish%22+AND+au:Murthy&start=0&max_results=3&id_list=" type="application/atom+xml"/>

  <opensearch:startIndex>0</opensearch:startIndex>
  <entry>
    <id>http://arxiv.org/abs/2411.04427v3</id>
    <title>One fish, two fish, but not the whole sea: Alignment reduces language models' conceptual diversity</title>
    <updated>2025-07-07T18:34:06Z</updated>
    <link href="https://arxiv.org/abs/2411.04427v3" rel="alternate" type="text/html"/>
    <link href="https://arxiv.org/pdf/2411.04427v3" rel="related" type="application/pdf" title="pdf"/>
    <summary>Researchers in social science and psychology have recently proposed using large language models (LLMs) as replacements for humans in behavioral research. In addition to arguments about whether LLMs accurately capture population-level patterns, this has raised questions about whether LLMs capture human-like conceptual diversity. Separately, it is debated whether post-training alignment (RLHF or RLAIF) affects models' internal diversity. Inspired by human studies, we use a new way of measuring the conceptual diversity of synthetically-generated LLM "populations" by relating the internal variability of simulated individuals to the population-level variability. We use this approach to evaluate non-aligned and aligned LLMs on two domains with rich human behavioral data. While no model reaches human-like diversity, aligned models generally display less diversity than their instruction fine-tuned counterparts. Our findings highlight potential trade-offs between increasing models' value alignment and decreasing the diversity of their conceptual representations.</summary>
    <category term="cs.CL" scheme="http://arxiv.org/schemas/atom"/>
    <published>2024-11-07T04:38:58Z</published>
    <arxiv:comment>17 pages, 10 figures; updated with publishing information</arxiv:comment>
    <arxiv:primary_category term="cs.CL"/>
    <arxiv:journal_ref>Proceedings of the 2025 Conference of the Nations of the Americas Chapter of the Association for Computational Linguistics: Human Language Technologies (Volume 1: Long Papers)</arxiv:journal_ref>
    <author>
      <name>Sonia K. Murthy</name>
    </author>
    <author>

    </author>

      <name>Jennifer Hu</name>

<feed xmlns:opensearch="http://a9.com/-/spec/opensearch/1.1/" xmlns:arxiv="http://arxiv.org/schemas/atom" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2005/Atom">
  <id>https://arxiv.org/api/rjNamoT2r6vX03Y7k/rycgLBLPc</id>
  <title>arXiv Query: search_query=ti:"Alignment as Distribution Learning" AND au:Yun&id_list=&start=0&max_results=3</title>
  <updated>2026-03-23T18:17:55Z</updated>
  <link href="https://arxiv.org/api/query?search_query=ti:%22Alignment+as+Distribution+Learning%22+AND+au:Yun&start=0&max_results=3&id_list=" type="application/atom+xml"/>
  <opensearch:itemsPerPage>3</opensearch:itemsPerPage>

  <opensearch:startIndex>0</opensearch:startIndex>
  <entry>
    <id>http://arxiv.org/abs/2506.01523v1</id>
    <title>Alignment as Distribution Learning: Your Preference Model is Explicitly a Language Model</title>
    <updated>2025-06-02T10:36:31Z</updated>
    <link href="https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.01523v1" rel="alternate" type="text/html"/>
    <link href="https://arxiv.org/pdf/2506.01523v1" rel="related" type="application/pdf" title="pdf"/>
    <summary>Alignment via reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) has become the dominant paradigm for controlling the quality of outputs from large language models (LLMs). However, when viewed as `loss + regularization,' the standard RLHF objective lacks theoretical justification and incentivizes degenerate, deterministic solutions, an issue that variants such as Direct Policy Optimization (DPO) also inherit. In this paper, we rethink alignment by framing it as \emph{distribution learning} from pairwise preference feedback by explicitly modeling how information about the target language model bleeds through the preference data. This explicit modeling leads us to propose three principled learning objectives: preference maximum likelihood estimation, preference distillation, and reverse KL minimization. We theoretically show that all three approaches enjoy strong non-asymptotic $O(1/n)$ convergence to the target language model, naturally avoiding degeneracy and reward overfitting. Finally, we empirically demonstrate that our distribution learning framework, especially preference distillation, consistently outperforms or matches the performances of RLHF and DPO across various tasks and models.</summary>
    <category term="cs.LG" scheme="http://arxiv.org/schemas/atom"/>
    <category term="stat.ML" scheme="http://arxiv.org/schemas/atom"/>
    <published>2025-06-02T10:36:31Z</published>
    <arxiv:comment>26 pages, 7 tables</arxiv:comment>
    <arxiv:primary_category term="cs.LG"/>
    <author>
      <name>Jihun Yun</name>
    </author>
    <author>

    </author>

  <updated>2026-03-23T18:17:56Z</updated>
  <link href="https://arxiv.org/api/query?search_query=ti:%22Diverse+Preference+Optimization%22+AND+au:Lanchantin&start=0&max_results=3&id_list=" type="application/atom+xml"/>

  <entry>
    <id>http://arxiv.org/abs/2501.18101v4</id>
    <title>Diverse Preference Optimization</title>
    <updated>2025-05-22T17:50:19Z</updated>
    <link href="https://arxiv.org/abs/2501.18101v4" rel="alternate" type="text/html"/>
    <link href="https://arxiv.org/pdf/2501.18101v4" rel="related" type="application/pdf" title="pdf"/>
    <summary>Post-training of language models, either through reinforcement learning, preference optimization or supervised finetuning, tends to sharpen the output probability distribution and reduce the diversity of generated responses. This is particularly a problem for creative generative tasks where varied responses are desired. In this work we introduce Diverse Preference Optimization (DivPO), an optimization method which learns to generate much more diverse responses than standard pipelines, while maintaining the quality of the generations. In DivPO, preference pairs are selected by first considering a pool of responses, and a measure of diversity among them, and selecting chosen examples as being more rare but high quality, while rejected examples are more common, but low quality. DivPO results in generating 45.6% more diverse persona attributes, and a 74.6% increase in story diversity, while maintaining similar win rates as standard baselines. On general instruction following, DivPO results in a 46.2% increase in diversity, and a 2.4% winrate improvement compared to DPO.</summary>
    <category term="cs.CL" scheme="http://arxiv.org/schemas/atom"/>
    <published>2025-01-30T02:47:41Z</published>

<feed xmlns:opensearch="http://a9.com/-/spec/opensearch/1.1/" xmlns:arxiv="http://arxiv.org/schemas/atom" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2005/Atom">
  <id>https://arxiv.org/api/yK9rkX9eRLtWSucDU1wYbEmbX+c</id>
  <title>arXiv Query: search_query=ti:"Creative Preference Optimization" AND au:Ismayilzada&id_list=&start=0&max_results=3</title>
  <updated>2026-03-23T18:17:57Z</updated>
  <link href="https://arxiv.org/api/query?search_query=ti:%22Creative+Preference+Optimization%22+AND+au:Ismayilzada&start=0&max_results=3&id_list=" type="application/atom+xml"/>
  <opensearch:itemsPerPage>3</opensearch:itemsPerPage>
  <opensearch:totalResults>1</opensearch:totalResults>
  <opensearch:startIndex>0</opensearch:startIndex>
  <entry>
    <id>http://arxiv.org/abs/2505.14442v2</id>
    <title>Creative Preference Optimization</title>
    <updated>2025-09-19T11:33:34Z</updated>
    <link href="https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.14442v2" rel="alternate" type="text/html"/>
    <link href="https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14442v2" rel="related" type="application/pdf" title="pdf"/>
    <summary>While Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated impressive performance across natural language generation tasks, their ability to generate truly creative content-characterized by novelty, diversity, surprise, and quality-remains limited. Existing methods for enhancing LLM creativity often focus narrowly on diversity or specific tasks, failing to address creativity's multifaceted nature in a generalizable way. In this work, we propose Creative Preference Optimization (CrPO), a novel alignment method that injects signals from multiple creativity dimensions into the preference optimization objective in a modular fashion. We train and evaluate creativity-augmented versions of several models using CrPO and MuCE, a new large-scale human preference dataset spanning over 200,000 human-generated responses and ratings from more than 30 psychological creativity assessments. Our models outperform strong baselines, including GPT-4o, on both automated and human evaluations, producing more novel, diverse, and surprising generations while maintaining high output quality. Additional evaluations on NoveltyBench further confirm the generalizability of our approach. Together, our results demonstrate that directly optimizing for creativity within preference frameworks is a promising direction for advancing the creative capabilities of LLMs without compromising output quality.</summary>
    <category term="cs.CL" scheme="http://arxiv.org/schemas/atom"/>
    <category term="cs.AI" scheme="http://arxiv.org/schemas/atom"/>
    <published>2025-05-20T14:43:41Z</published>
    <arxiv:comment>Accepted to EMNLP 2025 Findings</arxiv:comment>
    <arxiv:primary_category term="cs.CL"/>
    <author>
      <name>Mete Ismayilzada</name>
    </author>
    <author>
      <name>Antonio Laverghetta</name>
    </author>
    <author>
      <name>Simone A. Luchini</name>
    </author>

input.command:
source .venv/bin/activate && python3 << 'PYEOF'
import subprocess, os

# Papers that need re-downloading with correct arXiv IDs
papers = [
    ("2309.16240", "wang2023_f_dpo"),
    ("2310.06452", "kirk2023_rlhf_diversity"),
    ("2205.11275", "korbak2022_kl_bayesian_inference"),
    ("2406.06144", "ji2024_resist_alignment"),
    ("2312.01552", "lin2023_unlocking_base"),
    ("2406.17692", "lake2024_distributional_pluralism"),
    ("2402.13669", "yang2024_self_distillation"),
    ("2405.17931", "lu2024_online_merging"),
    ("2402.12343", "zhou2024_emulated_disalignment"),
    ("2503.04346", "zhu2025_alignment_control"),
    ("2406.00832", "gui2024_bonbon"),
    ("2404.01730", "yang2024_asymptotics_alignment"),
    ("2407.13399", "huang2024_chi_squared"),
    ("2406.05587", "mohammadi2024_creativity_debiasing"),
    ("2411.04427", "murthy2024_conceptual_diversity"),
    ("2506.01523", "yun2025_alignment_distribution"),
    ("2501.18101", "lanchantin2025_diverse_pref_opt"),
    ("2505.14442", "ismayilzada2025_creative_pref_opt"),
]

for arxiv_id, name in papers:
    url = f"https://arxiv.org/pdf/{arxiv_id}.pdf"
    path = f"papers/{name}.pdf"
    # Remove old wrong file
    if os.path.exists(path):
        os.remove(path)
    print(f"Downloading {name} ({arxiv_id})...")
    r = subprocess.run(["curl", "-sL", "-o", path, url], capture_output=True, timeout=60)
    if r.returncode != 0:
        print(f"  FAILED")
    else:
        size = os.path.getsize(path)
        print(f"  {'OK' if size > 10000 else 'WARNING: small'} ({size//1024} KB)")
PYEOF

## Comprehensive Summary: Kirk et al. (ICLR 2024) -- "Understanding the Effects of RLHF on LLM Generalisation and Diversity"

### 1. Diversity Metrics

The authors measure output diversity using three complementary metrics, all validated by Tevet & Berant (2021) as aligning well with human diversity judgments:

- **Expectation-Adjusted Distinct N-grams (EAD):** Counts distinct N-grams (averaged over n=1..5) in a set of outputs, using an expectation-adjusted formula to remove bias toward shorter outputs. Captures **syntactic diversity**.
- **Sentence-BERT Embedding Cosine Similarity:** Embeds each output using a sentence transformer, computes average pairwise cosine similarity, and reports 1 minus that similarity. Captures **semantic diversity**.
- **NLI Diversity:** Uses a natural language inference model to count entailments and contradictions between pairs of sentences sampled from outputs. More contradictions and fewer entailments = higher diversity. Captures **logical diversity**.

They measure diversity in two modes:
- **Per-input diversity:** For each of N=500 inputs, sample K=16 outputs at temperature 1, compute diversity within that set, then average across inputs. This measures how varied the model's outputs are for a single prompt.
- **Across-input diversity:** Take one output per input across all N inputs and compute diversity over that corpus. This measures whether the model produces similar text regardless of the input (i.e., mode collapse).

### 2. RLHF's Effect on Diversity vs. SFT

- RLHF **substantially reduces per-input diversity** compared to SFT across EAD and Sentence-BERT metrics. The drops are large and consistent.
- RLHF also reduces **across-input diversity**, though the difference is smaller. This is taken as the first rigorous empirical evidence of "across-input mode collapse" from RLHF -- even for different inputs, RLHF models tend to produce text of a similar style/mode.
- **NLI diversity** showed no meaningful difference between models in either per-input or across-input settings, meaning all models are similarly diverse in a logical sense.
- **Best-of-N (BoN)** has similar or higher across-input diversity than SFT for EAD and Sent-BERT, indicating the across-input diversity drop from RLHF cannot be explained purely by the reward model -- it is the PPO optimization that causes it.
- These diversity results are confirmed across OPT models at five scales (125M to 6.7B) in the summarisation task.
- For instruction following, diversity differences were not meaningfully detected, which the authors attribute to the metrics being designed for shorter outputs while instruction-following outputs are much longer (and RLHF models produce longer outputs, confounding the metrics).

### 3. Models, Datasets, and Tasks

**Models:**
- Primary: **LLaMA 7B** (decoder-only transformer)
- Additional: **OPT** at five sizes (125M, 350M, 1.3B, 2.7B, 6.7B) in appendix experiments

**Tasks and Datasets:**
- **Summarisation:** Trained on the filtered TL;DR dataset (~120K Reddit posts with summaries, ~64K preference comparisons from Stiennon et al. 2022). ID test: TL;DR test set. OOD test: CNN/DailyMail (news article summarisation).
- **Instruction Following:** Used AlpacaFarm models/data (Dubois et al. 2023), based on LLaMA 7B, with SFT data from text-davinci-003 outputs. ID test: regenerated AlpacaFarm Self-Instruct test set. OOD tests: (a) AlpacaEval (curated from diverse open-source instruction/dialogue datasets -- smaller distribution shift), (b) Sequential Instructions (custom dataset with multi-step compositional instructions -- larger distribution shift).
- Additional OPT experiments used split versions of TL;DR along length, sentiment, and subreddit dimensions.

**Methods compared:** SFT, RLHF (PPO), Best-of-N (BoN with N=16).

**Evaluation:** GPT-4 as simulated human annotator (validated against human preferences with ~69-74% agreement), reporting "Preference vs Reference" (PvR) win rates and head-to-head comparisons.

### 4. KL Penalty Coefficient's Effect on Diversity

The standard RLHF reward is: R(x,y) = RM(x,y) - beta_KL * KL(pi_RL || pi_SFT), with beta_KL = 0.05 used throughout.

The authors swept over beta_KL values of {0.01, 0.05, 0.5, 1.0}. The results were counterintuitive and important:

- **Increasing the KL penalty did NOT recover diversity.** Instead, higher KL penalties led to *lower* per-input diversity (not higher), while also causing worse performance.
- This means the KL penalty cannot serve as a simple knob to trade off between diversity and generalisation. The authors emphasize this shows more sophisticated methods are needed to address the diversity-generalisation tradeoff.

### 5. The Diversity-Generalisation Tradeoff

This is the paper's central finding:

- **RLHF generalises better than SFT**, particularly under larger distribution shifts. On the hardest OOD task (Sequential Instructions), RLHF's advantage over SFT grew substantially compared to ID or easier OOD settings. This may explain why RLHF models perform well in practice when users present highly varied, OOD queries.
- **But RLHF reduces output diversity** substantially compared to SFT.
- This creates an **inherent tension**: current fine-tuning techniques force a choice between better generalisation (RLHF) and higher output diversity (SFT).
- The authors suspect there exists a Pareto frontier of diversity vs. generalisation, but current methods are not yet on it. They suggest future work could draw on diversity-injection techniques from the deep RL literature (e.g., maximum entropy RL).
- BoN provides a partial middle ground: it achieves strong generalisation (limited by the SFT model and RM generalisation) while preserving more across-input diversity, but at high inference-time cost.

### 6. Style and Creative Capability Loss

- The across-input Sentence-BERT results show RLHF models consistently produce less semantically diverse text across different inputs, indicating a **tendency toward a particular output style or content mode** regardless of the input -- this is the "mode collapse" phenomenon hypothesized by janus (2022).
- At the per-input level, the diversity collapse is dramatic: RLHF models produce near-identical outputs when sampled multiple times for the same input, as shown by very low EAD and Sent-BERT scores.
- The paper explicitly notes implications for creative use cases: for story generation, red-teaming, and rejection sampling where diverse outputs are needed, SFT may be preferable. RLHF is better suited for chatbot deployment where generalisation to diverse user queries matters more than output variety.
- Diversity does not change much with model scale in their experiments.

### 7. Code and Data Availability

- **Code:** Open-sourced at https://github.com/facebookresearch/rlfh-gen-div
- **Sequential Instructions dataset:** Available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/UCL-DARK/sequential-instructions
- The paper uses existing public datasets (TL;DR, CNN/DailyMail, AlpacaFarm) and public model weights (LLaMA, OPT).

6. Implications for model interpolation

## Summary: "Emulated Disalignment" (Zhou et al., 2024)

### 1. Mathematical Formulation

The paper's core contribution is a closed-form, training-free method for reversing safety alignment. It builds on the DPO duality (Rafailov et al., 2024) between reward functions and language models:

- **Reward recovery:** Given a base model and its aligned version, the implicit safety reward is `r_align(x,y) = log pi_align(y|x) - log pi_base(y|x) + log Z(x)`. This reward penalizes harmful responses and encourages safe ones (empirically verified across all four model families).

- **Disalignment objective:** Instead of maximizing this reward (alignment), ED *minimizes* it with a KL constraint against the base model:
  `pi_disalign = argmax_pi E[-alpha * r_align(x,y) - KL(pi || pi_base)]`

- **Closed-form solution:** Combining the reward expression with the KL-constrained optimum yields a sequence-level distribution: `pi_disalign(y|x) proportional to pi_base(y|x)^{alpha+1} / pi_align(y|x)^alpha`.

- **Per-token approximation:** Since the sequence-level distribution is incompatible with autoregressive generation, they apply a per-token factorization (following EFT): `pi_ED(y_t | x, y_{<t}) proportional to pi_base(y_t | x, y_{<t})^{alpha+1} / pi_align(y_t | x, y_{<t})^alpha`. This is the actual sampling rule -- requiring only two forward passes per token.

### 2. Connection to Emulated Fine-Tuning (EFT) and Distribution Arithmetic

ED is the adversarial mirror image of EFT (Mitchell et al., 2023). EFT views alignment as KL-constrained reward maximization and shows one can "emulate" a larger aligned model by using a small aligned model's log-probability shift to guide a larger base model. ED inverts this: it uses the same log-probability contrast but with a negative sign on the reward, turning the alignment signal into a disalignment signal. The operation is pure distribution arithmetic at the logit level -- no gradients, no training, just combining two models' next-token distributions at inference time. The method also has a contrastive decoding interpretation: it amplifies behaviors present in the base model but suppressed by alignment.

### 3. Key Experimental Results

**Models tested:** Four families, all 7B scale -- Llama-1 (+ Vicuna), Llama-2 (+ Llama-2-chat), Mistral (+ Mistral-Instruct), Alpaca (+ Beaver). Additional scaling experiments on Llama-2-13B and Llama-2-70B.

**Attack success rates (harmful rate %):**
- ED achieved the highest harmful rate in **43 out of 48** evaluation subsets (4 families x 3 datasets x 2 query types x 2 evaluators).
- ED roughly **doubles the harmfulness** of pre-trained base models across the board. For example, Llama-2 base with malicious prompt: 21.3% avg harmful rate; ED: 37.0%. Alpaca base: 27.4%; ED: 57.6%.
- Safety-aligned models with malicious prompts are largely resistant (Llama-2-chat: 2.1%), but ED extracts and inverts that very resistance.
- Cross-family attacks work (e.g., using Llama-2-chat to attack Alpaca-base) as long as vocabularies match, though within-family attacks are stronger.
- Results are consistent across 7B, 13B, and 70B model sizes.

### 4. What This Reveals About Base Model Distributions Surviving Alignment

This is the paper's most consequential finding for the "keeping base models" thesis:

- **Alignment does not destroy the base distribution; it merely reweights it.** The entire ED method depends on the fact that `pi_align(y|x) = (1/Z) * pi_base(y|x) * exp(r_align(x,y))`. The base model's distribution is still fully recoverable from the aligned model given access to both sets of logits. Safety alignment is a thin, invertible layer on top of the base distribution.

- **Stronger alignment creates a larger exploitable gap.** The synthetic experiments (Section 6) definitively show: as the alignment strength (beta^{-1}) increases, the safety-aligned model becomes safer, but the *emulated disaligned model becomes proportionally more harmful*. The alignment signal is a double-edged sword -- it precisely identifies which tokens are "dangerous" and by how much, creating a roadmap for harm.

- **The base model's knowledge and capabilities are fully intact** under alignment. ED does not inject new knowledge; it merely redirects the generative capacity that was always present in the base model but suppressed by the alignment layer.

### 5. The Alpha Parameter

Alpha controls the trade-off between reward minimization (disalignment strength) and KL divergence from the base model (coherence):

- **alpha = 0:** Reduces to plain base model sampling (no disalignment).
- **Small alpha (e.g., 0.3):** Gentle disalignment; produces coherent, detailed harmful responses. This is where ED is most effective on well-aligned models like Llama-2-chat.
- **alpha = 1:** Symmetric to the alignment strength; provably emulates training-based disalignment with matched KL budget. Surprisingly outperforms actual RL-based direct disalignment in most regimes.
- **Large alpha (>1):** Amplifies the disalignment signal beyond the alignment budget. Can increase harmfulness further but risks "emulated reward over-optimization" -- responses degrade to nonsensical characters, repetitive text, or very brief outputs. This mirrors the reward over-optimization phenomenon in standard RLHF.
- **Practical values used:** alpha = 0.3 for Llama-2 and Mistral; alpha = 0.6 for Llama-1; alpha = 2.0 for Alpaca (which tolerates higher alpha because Alpaca is instruction-tuned and thus more resilient to deviation).

The key insight: for strongly aligned models, you need *less* alpha to achieve maximum harm. The alignment itself provides most of the signal.

### 6. Implications for Model Interpolation

- **Distribution arithmetic is powerful and dangerous.** The formula `pi_base^{alpha+1} / pi_align^alpha` is a simple logit-space operation that achieves results competitive with or exceeding expensive RL-based fine-tuning. This demonstrates that meaningful behavioral control can be achieved through pure interpolation/extrapolation in distribution space without any parameter updates.

- **Releasing both base and aligned models is inherently risky.** The paper explicitly argues that releasing a (base, aligned) pair with shared vocabulary gives adversaries everything needed to construct a maximally harmful model at essentially zero cost.

- **The interpolation is continuous and controllable.** Alpha provides a smooth dial from "aligned" behavior (negative alpha would strengthen alignment) through "base" behavior (alpha=0) to "disaligned" behavior (positive alpha). This reinforces the view that alignment, base behavior, and misalignment exist on a continuous spectrum in distribution space, mediated by a single scalar.

- **Token-level approximation is surprisingly effective.** Despite being a per-token approximation to a sequence-level objective (with only loosely bounded regret), ED outperforms actual sequence-level RL training in most regimes. This suggests that the distributional difference between base and aligned models is well-captured at the token level -- alignment does not create deeply entangled sequence-level structure that resists local decomposition.

Can increase harmfulness further but risks "emulated reward over-optimization" -- responses degrade to nonsensical characters, repetitive text, or very brief outputs. This mirrors the reward over-optimization phenomenon in standard RLHF.
- **Practical values used:** alpha = 0.3 for Llama-2 and Mistral; alpha = 0.6 for Llama-1; alpha = 2.0 for Alpaca (which tolerates higher alpha because Alpaca is instruction-tuned and thus more resilient to deviation).

The key insight: for strongly aligned models, you need *less* alpha to achieve maximum harm. The alignment itself provides most of the signal.

### 6. Implications for Model Interpolation

- **Distribution arithmetic is powerful and dangerous.** The formula `pi_base^{alpha+1} / pi_align^alpha` is a simple logit-space operation that achieves results competitive with or exceeding expensive RL-based fine-tuning. This demonstrates that meaningful behavioral control can be achieved through pure interpolation/extrapolation in distribution space without any parameter updates.

- **Releasing both base and aligned models is inherently risky.** The paper explicitly argues that releasing a (base, aligned) pair with shared vocabulary gives adversaries everything needed to construct a maximally harmful model at essentially zero cost.

- **The interpolation is continuous and controllable.** Alpha provides a smooth dial from "aligned" behavior (negative alpha would strengthen alignment) through "base" behavior (alpha=0) to "disaligned" behavior (positive alpha). This reinforces the view that alignment, base behavior, and misalignment exist on a continuous spectrum in distribution space, mediated by a single scalar.

- **Token-level approximation is surprisingly effective.** Despite being a per-token approximation to a sequence-level objective (with only loosely bounded regret), ED outperforms actual sequence-level RL training in most regimes. This suggests that the distributional difference between base and aligned models is well-captured at the token level -- alignment does not create deeply entangled sequence-level structure that resists local decomposition.

## Summary: "Language Models Resist Alignment" (Ji et al., 2024)

### 1. What is "Elasticity" of Post-Alignment Models?

Elasticity is the tendency of aligned language models to revert to the behavior distribution formed during pre-training upon further fine-tuning. It has two components:

- **Resistance**: Pre-trained models tend to retain their original distribution and resist alignment. Inverse alignment (moving back toward pre-training) is consistently easier (lower loss) than forward alignment (moving away from pre-training).
- **Rebound**: The more deeply a model has been aligned (i.e., the further it has been pushed from its pre-training distribution), the faster it snaps back to the pre-training distribution when fine-tuned in the opposite direction.

Formally, elasticity exists in a model-dataset pair if there is an algorithmically simple inverse operation g and a small dataset Db (where |Db| is much smaller than |Da|, the alignment dataset) that can revert the aligned model back to a state behaviorally close to the original pre-trained model.

### 2. Compression Theory Explanation for Why Alignment is Fragile

The authors model LLM training as lossless data compression, leveraging the equivalence between minimizing log-likelihood loss and minimizing compression rate. The key theoretical result (Theorem 4.2) shows that when a model trained on a large pre-training dataset Dp and a small alignment dataset Da is subjected to perturbation:

- The rate of change in normalized compression rates is **inversely proportional to dataset size**: the alignment dataset's compression rate degrades k times faster than the pre-training dataset's improves, where k = |Dp|/|Da| (typically orders of magnitude).
- This follows an analogy to **Hooke's Law** in a series spring system: F is proportional to |Di| times the change in KL divergence. The dataset size acts like a spring constant -- the larger dataset (pre-training) is the stiffer spring that deforms less, while the smaller dataset (alignment) is the weaker spring that deforms much more under the same perturbation force.
- Because pre-training corpora are orders of magnitude larger than alignment datasets, any fine-tuning perturbation disproportionately destroys alignment while barely affecting the pre-training distribution. This is the fundamental information-theoretic reason alignment is fragile.

### 3. Experimental Evidence

**Models tested**: Llama2-7B, Llama2-13B, Llama3-8B, Gemma-2B, Qwen series (0.5B, 4B, 7B), TinyLlama (with varying pre-training checkpoints at 0.1T-3.0T tokens).

**Tasks and datasets**:
- Helpfulness: Alpaca (instruction following)
- Truthfulness: TruthfulQA
- Safety: Beavertails (safe/unsafe conversation)
- Sentiment: IMDb (positive/negative generation)

**Measuring reversion**:
- **Resistance**: Compare training loss of forward alignment (earlier slice trained on later slice's data) vs. inverse alignment (later slice trained on earlier slice's data). Inverse alignment consistently has lower loss across all model/dataset/slice combinations (Table 1).
- **Rebound**: Models are first SFT'd with positive data (1K-10K samples), then fine-tuned with negative data (100-2K samples). Performance is measured via sentiment classifiers (Sentiment RoBERTa for IMDb) or safety cost models (for Beavertails). KL divergence between aligned and pre-trained models is also used as a distribution-level metric.
- **Alignment algorithms**: Rebound validated across SFT, RLHF/PPO, DPO, KTO, and SimPO -- all show the same pattern.

### 4. How Model Size Affects Elasticity

Elasticity **positively correlates with model size**. Experiments on Qwen 0.5B, 4B, and 7B show that as parameter count increases:
- The initial performance decline under negative fine-tuning is faster (stronger rebound)
- The subsequent decline is slower (stronger resistance near the pre-training distribution)
- This holds across both SFT and DPO, and in both forward and reverse fine-tuning directions

Elasticity also **positively correlates with pre-training data volume**. Using TinyLlama checkpoints at 0.1T, 0.5T, 1.0T, 2.0T, 2.5T, and 3.0T tokens: more pre-training data produces faster initial degradation and slower subsequent decline. At 0.1T, almost no resistance is observed; by 0.5T, resistance becomes notable. The critical threshold appears to lie between 0.1T and 0.5T.

### 5. Rate of Performance Decline: Rapid Then Slow

The performance trajectory follows a characteristic two-phase pattern:
- **Phase 1 (rapid decline)**: When the aligned model is far from the pre-training distribution, even a small amount of negative fine-tuning data causes steep performance drops. This is the rebound effect -- the model is being pulled back toward its pre-training attractor.
- **Phase 2 (slow stabilization)**: Once the model approaches the pre-training distribution, the rate of decline drops significantly and performance plateaus near the pre-trained baseline. This is the resistance effect -- the model has returned to its natural equilibrium and now resists further change.

Models aligned with more positive data (pushed further from pre-training) show steeper Phase 1 drops. For example, in KL divergence experiments (Table 3), a Llama2-7B model fine-tuned with 10K safe samples needs only 729 unsafe samples to revert to the pre-training distribution, while one fine-tuned with 1K safe samples needs 961 -- the more aligned model rebounds faster.

### 6. Implications for Preserving/Recovering Base Model Distributions

This paper provides strong evidence that **the base model distribution is remarkably persistent and recoverable**:

- **The pre-training distribution acts as a powerful attractor.** Alignment is essentially a thin, fragile overlay. Even non-malicious fine-tuning on unrelated tasks can inadvertently strip away alignment. The base distribution can be recovered with a dataset orders of magnitude smaller than what was used for alignment.
- **Larger, better-trained models are harder to permanently align** -- the very scaling that makes models more capable also makes their pre-training distributions more entrenched and resistant to modification.
- **Open-source safety implications are severe.** If alignment can be cheaply reversed, then releasing aligned model weights provides only superficial safety guarantees. The authors argue this could "drastically lower the barrier for jailbreaking aligned models."
- **Practical mitigation**: The authors suggest (a) balancing data volumes across alignment targets so no single objective has disproportionately small training data, (b) using the elasticity theorem to calculate required data ratios as a constrained optimization problem, and (c) developing "untunable alignment" methods that modify model parameters beyond superficial changes -- though concrete algorithms remain an open problem.

## Summary: "Adding Alignment Control to Language Models" (Zhu et al., 2025)

### 1. How the CLM Method Works: The Identity Layer Approach

CLM (Controllable Language Model) adds a single new decoder layer preceding all original layers of the transformer. This layer is created by duplicating the first layer of the original model and then initializing it as an **identity mapping** -- specifically, the output projection matrices (W_out in attention, W_down in MLP) are set to zero. Because of residual connections, this makes the entire added layer a pass-through at initialization: the input equals the output, so the original model's predicted distribution is completely preserved before any training begins.

During training, **only this single added layer is trained** (via DPO) while all original layers and the LM head remain frozen. This means only ~2% of parameters are updated for an 8B model. The added layer learns to map unaligned input token embeddings into an "aligned" embedding space. The frozen downstream layers then transform these aligned embeddings into token distributions using their intact language modeling capabilities. PCA visualization (Figure 5) confirms the added layer does not project into a higher dimension but rather shifts embeddings within the same space.

A key finding motivating this design: **bottom layers matter far more than top layers for preference learning.** Fine-tuning only the bottom-1 layer of Llama3.1-8B yields 24.8% on Arena-Hard, while fine-tuning only the top-1 layer yields just 8.2%. This asymmetry had been scarcely explored in prior work.

Adding more than one identity layer does not help -- performance with +2 or +3 layers was consistently worse than +1 under the same hyperparameters.

### 2. Inference-Time Interpolation Between Aligned and Unaligned

At inference, the input embedding is fed through **two parallel paths**: (a) the newly added aligned layer, and (b) the original first layer (unaligned). Both hidden states are propagated independently through all remaining layers and the LM head, producing aligned logits h_aligned and unaligned logits h_unaligned. These are merged via:

**output = lambda * h_aligned + (1 - lambda) * h_unaligned**

The coefficient lambda functions like a user-controllable "temperature knob" for alignment strength. When lambda = 0, the model reduces to the SFT (unaligned) model. When lambda = 1.0, it behaves as the fully aligned model. The architecture is compatible with vLLM, with the only overhead being an increased KV cache (since two paths share all upper layers).

This is architecturally distinct from prior methods like DeRa (Liu et al., 2024), which require loading **two full models** at inference time and combining their logits. CLM embeds both paths within a single model.

### 3. Interpolation and Extrapolation Phenomena

The paper observes clear, consistent patterns:

- **Interpolation (0 < lambda < 1):** Alignment strength falls between the SFT baseline and the fully aligned model. For example, on Arena-Hard with Llama3.1-8B, lambda=0.5 yields 14.0% vs. SFT's 3.6% and lambda=1.0's 24.4%.

- **Extrapolation (lambda > 1):** Alignment can be pushed **beyond** what full DPO fine-tuning achieves. Llama3.1-8B at lambda=2.0 reaches 34.3% on Arena-Hard, far surpassing full DPO's 26.2%. Qwen2.5-7B at lambda=1.5 reaches 50.8% on Arena-Hard vs. full DPO's 45.5%.

- **Reward hacking at excessive lambda:** The distillation-based verification (Figure 3) shows that beyond a certain lambda (roughly 4-6 depending on model), scores collapse due to reward hacking.

- **Multi-turn degradation:** A notable failure mode -- extrapolation hurts second-turn dialogue performance because the preference dataset (UltraFeedback) contains only single-turn data. Increasing lambda overfits to single-instruction following at the cost of contextual coherence. The authors show this can be mitigated by using different lambda values per turn (e.g., higher lambda for turn 1, lower for turn 2).

- **Math/RL setting:** The interpolation/extrapolation phenomenon also appears with rule-based sparse rewards on MATH500 with Qwen2.5-1.5B-chat, where lambda=0.5 actually outperforms lambda=1.0 (53.1% vs. 46.9%).

### 4. Key Experimental Results

**Models:** Llama3.2-3B, Llama3.1-8B, Qwen2.5-1.5B, Qwen2.5-7B (all base versions).

**Datasets:** UltraChat200k for SFT, UltraFeedback for DPO preference learning.

**Benchmarks and key numbers (CLM lambda=1.0 vs. full DPO):**

| Model | Benchmark | CLM (lambda=1.0) | Full DPO |
|-------|-----------|-------------------|----------|
| Llama3.2-3B | Arena-Hard | 11.0% | 11.6% |
| Llama3.1-8B | Arena-Hard | 24.4% | 26.2% |
| Qwen2.5-7B | Arena-Hard | 45.1% | 45.5% |
| Qwen2.5-7B | AlpacaEval2 LC | 25.7% | 25.2% |

CLM at lambda=1.0 matches full DPO despite training only ~2% of parameters. At lambda=1.5-2.0, CLM often **surpasses** full DPO.

**Reasoning benchmarks (Table 2):** MMLU, CMMLU, GSM8K show slight degradation as lambda increases, while TruthfulQA improves (since the preference data encodes honesty). The degradation is modest -- Llama3.1-8B MMLU goes from 59.78 (lambda=0) to 60.01 (lambda=2.0).

**Hyperparameter stability:** Smaller beta values (0.01, 0.001) work best; results are consistent across 3B and 8B scales.

### 5. Connection to Preserving Base Model Distributions

This paper is directly relevant to preserving base model distributions in several ways:

- **Architectural preservation:** By freezing all original layers and the LM head, CLM guarantees the base model's language modeling capabilities remain completely intact. The only modification is a prepended identity layer. At lambda=0, the model is exactly the SFT model.

- **Continuous access to the original distribution:** The dual-path inference architecture means the unaligned (reference) distribution is always available at runtime. The user can continuously interpolate between the base/SFT distribution and the aligned distribution, rather than being locked into one alignment strength.

- **Alignment as a separable transformation:** The paper demonstrates that alignment can be factored into a single-layer embedding-space transformation, cleanly separated from the model's core language modeling capabilities. This suggests alignment does not need to overwrite the base distribution -- it can be implemented as a controllable overlay.

- **Theoretical grounding in KL-regularized RL:** The interpolation formula (Eq. 7) formalizes the aligned model as lying on a continuum from the reference policy, governed by beta/lambda. This directly relates to the KL penalty that keeps the aligned model close to the base distribution -- lambda controls how far you move along this axis.

- **Mitigating alignment tax:** The reasoning results (Table 2) show that the base model's knowledge (MMLU, CMMLU) is largely preserved even at high lambda, precisely because the original layers are frozen and the base distribution remains accessible through the unaligned path.

| Qwen2.5-7B | AlpacaEval2 LC | 25.7% | 25.2% |

CLM at lambda=1.0 matches full DPO despite training only ~2% of parameters. At lambda=1.5-2.0, CLM often **surpasses** full DPO.

**Reasoning benchmarks (Table 2):** MMLU, CMMLU, GSM8K show slight degradation as lambda increases, while TruthfulQA improves (since the preference data encodes honesty). The degradation is modest -- Llama3.1-8B MMLU goes from 59.78 (lambda=0) to 60.01 (lambda=2.0).

**Hyperparameter stability:** Smaller beta values (0.01, 0.001) work best; results are consistent across 3B and 8B scales.

### 5. Connection to Preserving Base Model Distributions

This paper is directly relevant to preserving base model distributions in several ways:

- **Architectural preservation:** By freezing all original layers and the LM head, CLM guarantees the base model's language modeling capabilities remain completely intact. The only modification is a prepended identity layer. At lambda=0, the model is exactly the SFT model.

- **Continuous access to the original distribution:** The dual-path inference architecture means the unaligned (reference) distribution is always available at runtime. The user can continuously interpolate between the base/SFT distribution and the aligned distribution, rather than being locked into one alignment strength.

- **Alignment as a separable transformation:** The paper demonstrates that alignment can be factored into a single-layer embedding-space transformation, cleanly separated from the model's core language modeling capabilities. This suggests alignment does not need to overwrite the base distribution -- it can be implemented as a controllable overlay.

- **Theoretical grounding in KL-regularized RL:** The interpolation formula (Eq. 7) formalizes the aligned model as lying on a continuum from the reference policy, governed by beta/lambda. This directly relates to the KL penalty that keeps the aligned model close to the base distribution -- lambda controls how far you move along this axis.

- **Mitigating alignment tax:** The reasoning results (Table 2) show that the base model's knowledge (MMLU, CMMLU) is largely preserved even at high lambda, precisely because the original layers are frozen and the base distribution remains accessible through the unaligned path.

## Summary: "Online Merging Optimizers for Boosting Rewards and Mitigating Tax in Alignment" (Lu et al., 2024)

### 1. The Online Merging Approach

The paper introduces **Online Merging Optimizers** -- a method that integrates model merging into every RLHF optimization step rather than performing it once after training. The core idea: at each training step, the gradient-based weight update (delta-theta) is sparsified and then merged (via consensus) with the **delta parameters** of the reference SFT model (tau_r = theta_SFT - theta_base). This steers each gradient step toward maximizing reward *in the direction of SFT optimization*, preserving capabilities the SFT model already has.

Two concrete variants are proposed:
- **OnDARE**: Uses random (Bernoulli) sparsification of both the gradient update and the SFT delta, then linearly combines them with weight alpha.
- **OnTIES**: Uses top-k magnitude sparsification and sign-based consensus -- parameters with conflicting signs between the gradient and SFT delta are resolved by keeping the larger-magnitude one.

The update rule (relaxed form) is: `theta^(t+1) = theta^(t) + F(delta-theta) (consensus) F(tau_r)`, where F is sparsification and the consensus operator differs between OnDARE (linear combination) and OnTIES (sign agreement).

### 2. How It Differs from Post-Hoc (Offline) Model Averaging

**Offline merging** (e.g., WARM-style averaging, DARE, TIES, linear interpolation) combines the final RLHF model with the SFT model in a single shot after training concludes. The paper's preliminary experiments confirm that offline merging restores SFT-level performance on language benchmarks but at the cost of reduced preference rewards (MT-Bench/AlpacaEval scores drop significantly). This is because single-time parameter interpolation can only trade off between two fixed-capability endpoints -- it cannot find a point that exceeds both.

**Online merging** applies the merge at every optimization step, continuously regulating the training trajectory. This allows the optimizer to discover local minima that *simultaneously* preserve foundational capabilities and boost alignment reward -- something a one-shot interpolation cannot achieve.

The paper further bridges the two with **Step-K Online Merging**: merge every K steps instead of every step. K=1 is fully online; K=total_steps is equivalent to offline merging. Experiments show that as K increases, MT-Bench scores degrade toward vanilla AdamW levels, confirming that "onlineness" is critical for reward boosting.

### 3. The Alignment Tax Addressed

"Alignment tax" refers to the degradation of pre-trained and SFT-acquired capabilities during RLHF, including:
- Declining performance on NLP benchmarks (math, coding, reading comprehension, knowledge, instruction following)
- Loss of fundamental language skills
- Unexpected code-switching between languages
- Response length inflation

The paper shows that tuning the KL penalty (beta in DPO) cannot resolve this: higher beta preserves benchmarks but sacrifices preference scores; lower beta does the opposite. Online merging provides an orthogonal regularization that is **complementary** to KL constraints -- even under extremely low beta (1e-3), OnTIES maintains MT-Bench above the SFT reference level while AdamW collapses.

### 4. Key Experimental Results

**Models tested**: Qwen1.5-1.8B, Qwen1.5-7B, LLaMA-3-8B (three backbone families, 1.8B to 8B scale).

**RLHF algorithms**: DPO (primary), IPO, KTO -- online merging works across all.

**Benchmarks**: 12 benchmarks across 7 categories (Math: GSM8K, Math401, Math23K; Coding: HumanEval, MBPP, DS1000, CodeApex; Instruction Following: IFEval; Reading Comprehension: COQA, DROP; Knowledge: MMLU; Agent: NousResearch; Code-switching) plus MT-Bench and AlpacaEval 2.0 (LC).

**Key numbers (OnDARE vs. vanilla AdamW)**:
- **Qwen1.5-1.8B**: +0.5 benchmark avg, +0.24 MT-Bench, +0.05 AlpacaEval
- **Qwen1.5-7B**: +1.1 benchmark avg, +0.12 MT-Bench, +0.28 AlpacaEval
- **LLaMA-3-8B**: +1.3 benchmark avg, +0.19 MT-Bench, +1.57 AlpacaEval

Regularization baselines (KL penalty, EMA, ChildTuning, LoRA) and offline merging baselines (Linear, DARE, TIES) generally fail to improve both benchmark averages and preference scores simultaneously. OnDARE is the only method that consistently improves all three metrics across all three model families.

### 5. How Interpolation with SFT Model Preserves Capabilities During RLHF

The mechanism works at the parameter level through three complementary effects:

1. **Direction steering**: The SFT delta parameters (theta_SFT - theta_base) encode the "direction" of SFT optimization -- the capabilities learned during supervised fine-tuning. By merging this direction into every gradient update, the optimizer ensures that each RLHF step moves toward reward maximization while staying aligned with the SFT capability manifold.

2. **Sparsification as noise filtering**: At each step, the gradient update is sparsified (keeping only a fraction p of parameters). Remarkably, training remains stable even when discarding 99.95% of gradient modifications per step (p = 5e-4). This suggests most per-step gradient changes are redundant or harmful to base capabilities, and sparsification filters them out.

3. **Consensus as conflict resolution**: When the RLHF gradient conflicts with the SFT delta (e.g., pushing a parameter in the opposite direction from what SFT learned), the consensus operation (sign-based for OnTIES, weighted linear for OnDARE) resolves these conflicts in favor of preserving SFT capabilities while still allowing reward improvement.

The net effect is that the optimizer produces "well-calibrated gradients that balance reward maximization and alignment tax" -- the model continually improves on human preference metrics without drifting away from the competencies established during SFT and pre-training.

## Summary: Lake et al. (2024), "From Distributional to Overton Pluralism"

### 1. Distributional Shift Post-Alignment: Definition and Measurement

The paper defines post-alignment distributional shift as the change in an LLM's output distribution after SFT, RLHF, or DPO. The authors measure this shift along two axes:

- **Lexical diversity (Self-Sim):** Jaccard similarity over stemmed word sets, averaged across all pairs of N=5 sampled responses per input. Higher Self-Sim means less diversity. Llama 2 Base has Self-Sim of 0.17 on ConflictingQA; Llama 2 Chat rises to 0.36. This confirms reduced surface-level diversity post-alignment.

- **Response quality (5 dimensions):** Helpfulness, Clarity, Factuality, Depth, and Engagement, each scored 1-5 by GPT-4 using the Just-Eval protocol.

- **Coverage metrics:** Cover-LEX (Jaccard between base and aligned responses on the same input) and Cover-SEM (GPT-4 judged semantic coverage on a 1-5 scale) measure how much information from base model responses is "missing" in aligned model responses.

- **Stance analysis:** On binary questions (ConflictingQA), responses are classified as yes/no/both/other, and stance entropy is computed per question across samples.

The core finding is that alignment shifts diversity from being *distributional* (diverse information spread across multiple samples) to *Overton* (diverse information aggregated within a single, longer response). Aligned models produce responses roughly 10x longer than base models (412 vs 37 tokens on ConflictingQA), and these longer responses cover content that the base model only surfaces across many separate samples.

### 2. Does Alignment Suppress Useful Information?

**No.** The paper finds little evidence that alignment suppresses useful information from the base model. The key evidence:

- When coverage metrics are low (base model response contains information not in the aligned response), the base response quality also tends to be low. The "missing" information is predominantly low-quality, irrelevant, or unhelpful content (e.g., JSON blobs, off-topic continuations).

- On ConflictingQA, aligned models shift toward "both" stances (presenting multiple perspectives in a single response) rather than suppressing one viewpoint. The base model produces scattered yes/no responses across samples, while the aligned model consolidates both perspectives.

- The authors' main hypothesis -- "the only information missing from the aligned response is information that is not useful" -- is supported by the data.

### 3. Can Aligned Model Behavior Be Recovered Without Fine-Tuning?

**Yes.** This is the paper's second major contribution. Using progressively sophisticated in-context learning (ICL) strategies, the authors show base models can produce responses that match the self-similarity of aligned models to themselves:

- **Static prompts:** URIAL with human or teacher demonstrations improves quality substantially.
- **Dynamic prompts:** kNN-retrieved in-context examples (selected by query similarity) further close the gap.
- **Oracle prompts with semantic hints:** Providing GPT-4-generated summaries of the teacher's response as "hints" in the prompt achieves Max-Sim scores nearly equivalent to the aligned model's own Self-Sim (0.33 vs 0.34 on LIMA-OE; 0.31 vs 0.36 on ConflictingQA).

The metric used is Max-Sim: the maximum Jaccard similarity between N=5 student (prompted base) samples and a single teacher (aligned) response. The rationale is that if the best base model sample is as close to the aligned output as aligned outputs are to each other, the base model's distribution covers the aligned model's behavior.

### 4. Evidence For/Against the Superficial Alignment Hypothesis

The paper provides **strong evidence in favor** of the Superficial Alignment Hypothesis (Zhou et al., 2024):

- Alignment does not introduce new capabilities or information; it amplifies an existing subdistribution of assistant-like responses already present in the base model.
- The aligned model's output distribution corresponds to the "useful output subdistribution" of the base model.
- In-context alignment (no parameter updates) can closely replicate fine-tuned alignment behavior, demonstrating that the base model already contains the necessary capacity.
- Quality improvements from alignment (helpfulness, depth, engagement) can be substantially reproduced via ICL, with kNN-teacher prompting on LIMA-OE reaching 4.74 helpfulness vs 4.94 for Llama 2 Chat.

### 5. Implications for Base Model Distribution Preservation

This paper is directly relevant to arguments for preserving base model distributions:

- **Base models contain the full information.** Aligned model behavior is a strict subset of base model capabilities, not an extension of them. Current alignment "captures but does not extend" the useful base model subdistribution.
- **Alignment is lossy in diversity, not in information.** The reduction in distributional pluralism is a filtering/aggregation operation, not a destruction of knowledge.
- **In-context alignment as an alternative to parameter updates.** The success of ICL-based alignment suggests that base models can serve as flexible substrates for multiple alignment strategies without permanent parameter modification, enabling rapid prototyping and personalization.
- **The base distribution is recoverable.** Since alignment merely reweights the output distribution toward a useful subset, the original distribution can be accessed by prompting the base model appropriately.

### 6. Key Datasets and Experimental Setup

**Datasets:**
- **ConflictingQA** (Wan et al., 2024): 434 short controversial binary questions (avg 8 words). Examples: "Is the Gender Wage Gap a Myth?", "Were there dinosaurs on Noah's Ark?"
- **LIMA-OE**: 50 open-ended questions manually filtered from LIMA (Zhou et al., 2024), sourced from Reddit Q&A forums (avg 19 words). Examples: "Why can parrots talk?", "How can I improve my time management skills?"

**Models:**
- Llama 2 Base (7B), Llama 2 Chat (7B), Llama 2 with URIAL ICL
- Mistral Base (7B), Mistral Instruct (7B)
- GPT-3.5 Turbo (gpt-3.5-turbo-0613)

**Setup:**
- Generation: temperature=0.5, repetition penalty=1.1, max 768 tokens, N=5 samples per input
- Evaluation: GPT-4 (gpt-4-1106-preview) at temperature=0 for all automated assessments
- Lexical similarity: NLTK tokenization, stop word removal, stemming, then Jaccard similarity
- Embedding model for kNN retrieval: intfloat/e5-mistral-7b-instruct
- Hardware: single A10 GPU (24GB VRAM)

Here are the search results for your query about HuggingFace UltraFeedback, Anthropic-HH preference datasets, DPO, and RLHF training:

## Anthropic-HH Dataset

The Anthropic/hh-rlhf dataset contains human preference data about helpfulness and harmlessness from "Training a Helpful and Harmless Assistant with Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback," and these data are meant to train preference (or reward) models for subsequent RLHF training.

The Trelis/hh-rlhf-dpo dataset is built from Anthropic's hh-rlhf dataset and has been split into prompt, chosen and rejected rows, as required by HuggingFace's DPO trainer.

## DPO Training

Fine-tuning a language model via DPO involves data collection (gathering a preference dataset with positive and negative selected pairs of generation given a prompt) and exceeds PPO-based RLHF in controlling sentiment of generations while matching or improving response quality and being substantially simpler to implement and train.

## UltraFeedback with DPO

The HuggingFace DPO trainer uses preference data from the UltraFeedback dataset, with examples showing how to train models like Qwen2 0.5B on the ultrafeedback_binarized dataset.

The search results show that both the Anthropic-HH and UltraFeedback datasets are popular choices for preference-based training methods like DPO and RLHF in the HuggingFace ecosystem.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about these topics:

## TL;DR Summarization Dataset and RLHF

The TL;DR summarization work is a seminal RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) project by OpenAI where researchers created an RLHF pipeline from scratch and enumerated over 20 key implementation details.

The TL;DR summarization dataset has been used in RLHF research, where models trained with algorithms like DR-PO have been compared against PPO and DPO baselines.

## AlpacaFarm

AlpacaFarm is one of several datasets used for evaluating model alignment approaches, alongside others like UltraFeedback, GSM8K, HH-RLHF, and TutorEval.

## Related RLHF Datasets on HuggingFace

The HH-RLHF dataset contains human preference data about helpfulness and harmlessness from the "Training a Helpful and Harmless Assistant with Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback" work, meant to train preference (or reward) models for subsequent RLHF training.

The search results show these are active areas of research for fine-tuning language models using human feedback, with multiple implementations and datasets available on the HuggingFace Hub.

I found several relevant HuggingFace datasets and resources related to text diversity, style measurement, and authorship attribution. Here are the key findings:

## Key Datasets

The Victorian-Era-Authorship-Attribution dataset processes texts by removing words outside the top 10,000 words while keeping sentence structure intact, and splits entire books into text fragments with 1000 words each.

The style_change_detection dataset has a goal to identify text positions within a given multi-author document at which the author switches, which is a crucial part of the authorship identification process and multi-author document analysis.

The Blog Authorship Corpus consists of the collected posts of 19,320 bloggers gathered from blogger.com in August 2004, with a total of 681,288 posts and over 140 million words.

The guardian_authorship dataset is a dataset for cross-topic authorship attribution.

## Related Research

Synthetic dataset creation through prompting enables the training of interpretable style representations called LISA embeddings for use in authorship attribution.

AuthorMix is a large set of 30K high-quality, long-form texts from a diverse set of 14 authors and 4 domains.

Characters are the smallest unit of text that can extract stylometric signals to determine the author of a text.

These resources cover various approaches to measuring text diversity, analyzing writing style, and performing authorship attribution using HuggingFace datasets.

Here are the key findings from the web search about LLM evaluation benchmark datasets on HuggingFace:

## Benchmark Datasets Overview

MMLU, ARC, HellaSwag, and TruthfulQA are common benchmarks used for evaluating language models. These datasets are available through HuggingFace and are widely used in the community.

## Individual Benchmark Descriptions

**MMLU (Massive Multitask Language Understanding):**
MMLU is a test to measure a text model's multitask accuracy, covering 57 tasks including elementary mathematics, US history, computer science, law, and more.

**HellaSwag:**
HellaSwag is a test of commonsense inference, which is easy for humans (~95%) but challenging for SOTA models.

**TruthfulQA:**
TruthfulQA is a test to measure a model's propensity to reproduce falsehoods commonly found online.

## HuggingFace Integration

HuggingFace Datasets can load benchmark data like cais/mmlu, allenai/ai2_arc, Rowan/hellaswag, truthful_qa, and others. The Open LLM Leaderboard evaluates on ARC, HellaSwag, TruthfulQA, and MMLU, which provides a centralized location for comparing model performance across these benchmarks.

Based on the search results, here's information about the openbmb UltraFeedback binarized dataset:

## Original UltraFeedback Dataset

The original UltraFeedback dataset consists of 64k prompts, 256k responses and 380k high-quality feedback.

## UltraFeedback Binarized Versions

The binarized versions of UltraFeedback are specifically prepared for DPO training and other preference modeling tasks. Here are the key sizes:

**HuggingFaceH4/ultrafeedback_binarized:**
- Train preferences (train_prefs): 61,135 examples
- Train supervised fine-tuning (train_sft): 61,135 examples
- Test preferences (test_prefs): 200 examples

**Argilla's ultrafeedback-binarized-preferences-cleaned:**
- Training split: 60,917 examples with 284,937,773 bytes total dataset size

## Purpose for DPO

To create UltraFeedback Binarized, the highest overall_score was picked as the "chosen" completion, and one of the remaining 3 at random as the "rejected" one, defining the preference modelling splits for techniques like reward modelling or DPO.

I found several relevant datasets and resources on HuggingFace related to writing prompts and creative writing. Here are the key findings:

## Writing Prompt Datasets

The WritingPrompts dataset, as used in Hierarchical Neural Story Generation, is available on HuggingFace. Additionally, the Writing Prompt Augmentation Dataset is an extension of the FAIR writing prompt dataset, built to expand samples for feeding Open Assistant.

## Style and Genre Diversity

The literary-genre-examples dataset contains 86 fiction and nonfiction genres, each accompanied by a representative example paragraph that illustrate the typical tone, writing style, and content characteristics for each genre.

For more comprehensive style diversity, the LongPage dataset contains 6,000 books with plans to scale up to many more books, creating the largest reasoning-enhanced creative writing dataset ever assembled.

## AI-Generated Writing Datasets

One dataset contains 3,746 short stories generated with OpenAI's chatgpt-4o-latest model using Reddit's Writing Prompts subreddit as a source, with each sample generally between 6,000-8,000 characters long.

These datasets vary in size, style diversity, and source material, offering different options depending on your specific creative writing goals.

Based on the search results, I found information about the Anthropic hh-rlhf dataset structure, but the specific numbers for total dataset size and detailed train/test splits weren't fully displayed in these results. However, I can share what was found:

The dataset is approximately 70.4M in size (according to the Hugging Face dataset card). 

For the helpfulness subset, the data are grouped into train/test splits in three tranches: from base models (context-distilled 52B language models), via rejection sampling (mostly with best-of-16 sampling) against an early preference model, and a dataset sampled during the iterated "online" process.

Each line of the jsonl files contains a pair of texts, one "chosen" and one "rejected".

For more detailed information about specific example counts in each split, you may need to consult the original GitHub repository at https://github.com/anthropics/hh-rlhf or the paper "Training a Helpful and Harmless Assistant with Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback."

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Project Gutenberg datasets on HuggingFace related to author style and literary texts:

## Key Datasets Found:

**1. Despina/project_gutenberg (ARF Dataset)**
The Artificial Relationships in Fiction (ARF) is a synthetically annotated dataset for relation extraction in fiction, created from literary texts sourced from Project Gutenberg, capturing implicit relationships within fictional narratives using GPT-4o for annotation. It includes a metadata-rich corpus of 6,322 public domain fiction books (1850–1950) with inferred author gender and thematic categorization.

**2. contextlab/dickens-corpus**
This corpus includes 14 books by Charles Dickens with all text converted to lowercase and cleaned of Project Gutenberg headers, footers, and chapter headings to focus on the author's prose style.

**3. Fhrozen/gutenberg8k**
The Gutenberg dataset represents a corpus of over 15,000 book texts, their authors and titles, scraped from the Project Gutenberg website using a custom script to parse all bookshelves.

**4. laion/Project-Gutenberg**
This dataset provides bulk downloads of Gutenberg books in ten different languages, and is released for researchers to integrate these books for AI projects such as embeddings, text-generation and fine-tuning.

**5. agentlans/project-gutenberg-top100**
This dataset contains the 100 most downloaded Project Gutenberg eBooks with each eBook processed to extract clean, paragraph-level text suitable for natural language processing tasks.

These datasets offer various approaches to working with literary texts from Project Gutenberg, with options for author-specific analysis, style study, and broader textual research.

Based on the search results, I found details about the Victorian era authorship attribution dataset on HuggingFace:

The dataset was created to build the largest authorship attribution dataset by extracting works of 50 well-known authors. 

For training, there are 45 authors, whereas in testing there are 50 authors. Each instance in the training set consists of a text piece of 1000 words with an author id attached, while in the testing set there is only the text piece of 1000 words to perform authorship attribution.

The dataset is available on HuggingFace under the repository "NicholasSynovic/Victorian-Era-Authorship-Attribution." The source material comes from research titled "Benchmarking Authorship Attribution Techniques Using Over A Thousand Books by Fifty Victorian Era Novelists" by Abdulmecit Gungor from Purdue University (2018).
